docs.title: Sorting Techniques
docs.text:
Sorting means putting things in order, usually from smallest to biggest.

Example:

Before: 8 3 5 1 4
After : 1 3 4 5 8

Think of sorting like arranging books from shortest to tallest, or lining up students by height.

docs.title: Files In This Folder
docs.text:
• Bubble Sort
  • Insertion Sort
  • Selection Sort
  • Quick Sort
  • Merge Sort
  • Heap Sort
  • Comparison
  • Visual Comparison

docs.title: Fast Idea Of Each Method
docs.text: Algorithm Main Idea Bubble Sort Big items "bubble" to the end by swapping neighbors Insertion Sort Put each new item into the correct place in the sorted part Selection Sort Repeatedly pick the smallest item and put it in front Quick Sort Pick a pivot, split into smaller and bigger parts, sort them Merge Sort Split into halves, sort them, then merge back together Heap Sort Build a special tree shape and repeatedly remove the largest

docs.title: Which Ones Are Usually Faster?
docs.text:
For very small lists, Insertion Sort is often good.

For large lists:

  • Quick Sort is usually very fast in practice
  • Merge Sort is very reliable and always O(n log n) time
  • Heap Sort is also O(n log n) time, but often a bit less friendly to understand

docs.title: Easy Memory Trick
docs.text:
• O(n^2) sorts: Bubble, Insertion, Selection
  • O(n log n) sorts: Quick (average), Merge, Heap

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
For the side-by-side table and when to use each one, read Comparison.

For diagram-heavy visuals, read Visual Comparison.

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
Imagine numbers are standing in a line.

You look at two neighbors at a time:

  • If they are in the wrong order, swap them
  • Keep doing that again and again

The biggest number keeps moving right, like a bubble floating up in water.

docs.title: Small Example
docs.text:
Sort:

5 3 8 1

Pass 1:

5 3 8 1
3 5 8 1   swap 5 and 3
3 5 8 1   5 and 8 are fine
3 5 1 8   swap 8 and 1

Now the biggest number, 8, is in the correct place.

Pass 2:

3 5 1 8
3 5 1 8   3 and 5 are fine
3 1 5 8   swap 5 and 1

Pass 3:

3 1 5 8
1 3 5 8   swap 3 and 1

Sorted.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
Start:   [5] [3] [8] [1]
          |---|
Swap ->  [3] [5] [8] [1]

Then move right:
               |---|
No swap -> [3] [5] [8] [1]

Then move right:
                    |---|
Swap ->     [3] [5] [1] [8]

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Compare neighboring elements
  2. Swap if left is bigger than right
  3. After one full pass, the biggest unsorted element reaches the end
  4. Repeat for the remaining unsorted part

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
bubbleSort(arr):
    repeat n-1 times:
        swapped = false
        for i from 0 to n-2:
            if arr[i] > arr[i+1]:
                swap arr[i], arr[i+1]
                swapped = true
        if swapped == false:
            stop early

docs.title: Time Complexity
docs.text: Case Time Best O(n) Average O(n^2) Worst O(n^2)

docs.title: Best Case: O(n)
docs.text:
If the list is already sorted, we make one pass, see no swaps, and stop.

That means we check about n elements once.

docs.title: Average And Worst Case: O(n^2)
docs.text:
In the average or bad case, we may do:

  • about n passes
  • and in each pass, about n comparisons

So it becomes:

n * n = n^2

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text: O(1) because it sorts in the same array and uses only a tiny amount of extra memory.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Very easy to understand
  • Very slow for big lists
  • Good for learning, not usually for real large programs

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text:
• When you are just starting to learn sorting
  • When the list is tiny
  • When you want a simple idea, not the fastest one

docs.title: Big Table
docs.text: Algorithm Best Time Average Time Worst Time Extra Space Main Idea Bubble Sort O(n) O(n^2) O(n^2) O(1) Swap neighbors again and again Insertion Sort O(n) O(n^2) O(n^2) O(1) Insert each item into the sorted part Selection Sort O(n^2) O(n^2) O(n^2) O(1) Repeatedly choose the smallest Quick Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n^2) O(log n) average stack Split around a pivot Merge Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n) Split, sort halves, merge Heap Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(1) Use a heap to remove largest repeatedly

docs.title: Which One Is Easiest?
docs.text:
Usually:

  1. Bubble Sort
  2. Selection Sort
  3. Insertion Sort
  4. Merge Sort
  5. Quick Sort
  6. Heap Sort

Heap Sort is often harder to picture because of the heap tree idea.

docs.title: Which One Is Fastest In Real Life?
docs.text:
There is no single winner for every situation, but usually:

  • Quick Sort is very fast on average
  • Merge Sort is very dependable
  • Heap Sort is good when you want O(n log n) without extra array memory
  • Insertion Sort is great for very small or nearly sorted lists

docs.title: Bubble Sort
docs.text:
• Very easy
  • Very slow
  • Good for learning

docs.title: Insertion Sort
docs.text:
• Feels like sorting cards in your hand
  • Good for small or almost sorted lists

docs.title: Selection Sort
docs.text:
• Simple idea
  • Always does lots of checking

docs.title: Quick Sort
docs.text:
• Usually very fast
  • Can become bad if pivot choice is poor

docs.title: Merge Sort
docs.text:
• Always nicely fast
  • Needs extra memory

docs.title: Heap Sort
docs.text:
• Always nicely fast
  • Harder to understand than Merge Sort or Quick Sort

docs.title: Why O(n^2) Is Slower Than O(n log n)
docs.text:
Imagine n = 1000.

  • n^2 = 1000000
  • n log n is much smaller

So when the list gets big, O(n log n) algorithms usually win by a lot.

docs.title: Simple Race Analogy
docs.text:
Small race:
Insertion Sort can do okay

Big race:
Bubble / Selection / Insertion fall behind
Quick / Merge / Heap move ahead

docs.title: Quick Picking Guide
docs.text:
Choose Bubble Sort if:

  • you are learning basics

Choose Insertion Sort if:

  • the array is small
  • the array is nearly sorted

Choose Selection Sort if:

  • you want a simple method with few swaps

Choose Quick Sort if:

  • you want strong average performance

Choose Merge Sort if:

  • you want guaranteed fast time
  • stability matters

Choose Heap Sort if:

  • you want guaranteed fast time
  • and low extra memory

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
Heap Sort uses a special tree shape called a heap.

In a max-heap:

  • the biggest number is always at the top

So Heap Sort does this:

  1. Build a max-heap
  2. Take the biggest element from the top
  3. Put it at the end
  4. Fix the heap
  5. Repeat

docs.title: Small Example
docs.text:
Sort:

4 10 3 5 1

After building a max-heap, the biggest value goes to the top.

One possible heap view:

        10
       /  \
      5    3
     / \
    4   1

Now swap the top with the last element:

10 goes to the end

Then fix the heap again with the remaining elements.

Keep repeating until sorted.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
Max-Heap:
        9
       / \
      7   6
     / \
    3   2

Largest is always at the top.
Move it to the end, then rebuild the top.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Convert the array into a max-heap
  2. Swap the first element with the last unsorted element
  3. Reduce heap size by one
  4. Heapify again
  5. Repeat

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
heapSort(arr):
    buildMaxHeap(arr)
    for end from n-1 down to 1:
        swap arr[0], arr[end]
        heapify(arr, size = end, root = 0)

docs.title: Time Complexity
docs.text: Case Time Best O(n log n) Average O(n log n) Worst O(n log n)

docs.title: Why This Time Complexity?
docs.text:
Building the heap takes O(n).

Then we remove the largest element n times, and each time fixing the heap takes about O(log n).

So total work is about:

n * log n

That gives O(n log n).

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text: O(1) extra space for the common in-place version.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Good guaranteed speed
  • Does not need extra array space like Merge Sort
  • Usually not as fast in practice as a good Quick Sort

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text:
• When you want guaranteed O(n log n) time
  • When you also want in-place sorting

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
Imagine you are holding playing cards.

You pick one card at a time and slide it into the correct spot among the cards already in your hand.

That is exactly how Insertion Sort works.

docs.title: Small Example
docs.text:
Sort:

5 3 8 1

Start:

[5] | 3 8 1

[5] is the sorted part.

Insert 3:

3 5 | 8 1

Insert 8:

3 5 8 | 1

Insert 1:

1 3 5 8

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
Sorted part   Unsorted part
[5]           [3] [8] [1]

Take 3 and insert it:
[3] [5]       [8] [1]

Take 8 and insert it:
[3] [5] [8]   [1]

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Pretend the first element is already sorted
  2. Take the next element
  3. Move bigger elements one step right
  4. Put the current element in its correct place
  5. Repeat

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
insertionSort(arr):
    for i from 1 to n-1:
        key = arr[i]
        j = i - 1
        while j >= 0 and arr[j] > key:
            arr[j + 1] = arr[j]
            j = j - 1
        arr[j + 1] = key

docs.title: Time Complexity
docs.text: Case Time Best O(n) Average O(n^2) Worst O(n^2)

docs.title: Best Case: O(n)
docs.text:
If the list is already sorted, each new item is already in the right place.

So we only do one quick check for each element.

docs.title: Average Case: O(n^2)
docs.text:
Usually, each new element must move left a little bit.

That creates many shifts over and over.

docs.title: Worst Case: O(n^2)
docs.text:
If the list is in reverse order, every new element must move across almost the whole sorted part.

Example:

5 4 3 2 1

The total work becomes:

1 + 2 + 3 + ... + (n-1)

That adds up to about n^2.

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text: O(1) because it sorts in place.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Great for very small lists
  • Great when the list is already almost sorted
  • Slow for large random lists

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text:
• Small arrays
  • Nearly sorted data
  • As a helper inside bigger sorting systems

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
Imagine tearing a big pile of cards into smaller and smaller piles until every pile has just one card.

Then you join the piles back together in sorted order.

That is Merge Sort.

docs.title: Small Example
docs.text:
Sort:

5 3 8 1

Split:

5 3 | 8 1
5 | 3   8 | 1

Single items are already sorted.

Now merge:

5 and 3  -> 3 5
8 and 1  -> 1 8

Merge the two sorted parts:

3 5 and 1 8 -> 1 3 5 8

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Split the array into two halves
  2. Sort each half
  3. Merge the two sorted halves
  4. Repeat until complete

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
mergeSort(arr):
    if array size <= 1:
        return

    split into left and right
    mergeSort(left)
    mergeSort(right)
    merge(left, right)

docs.title: Time Complexity
docs.text: Case Time Best O(n log n) Average O(n log n) Worst O(n log n)

docs.title: Why This Time Complexity?
docs.text:
Merge Sort always:

  • splits into halves, which creates about log n levels
  • merges all n elements at each level

So the work is:

n * log n

No matter if the input is already sorted or completely messy, the time stays the same.

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text: O(n) because it usually needs extra arrays while merging.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Very reliable
  • Good for large data
  • Stable sort
  • Needs extra memory

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text:
• When you want guaranteed O(n log n) time
  • When stability matters
  • When extra memory is okay

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
Pick one number and call it the pivot.

Now split everything into:

  • numbers smaller than the pivot
  • the pivot
  • numbers bigger than the pivot

Then do the same thing again for the left part and the right part.

This is called divide and conquer.

docs.title: Small Example
docs.text:
Sort:

5 3 8 1 4

Pick pivot = 5

Split:

Smaller: 3 1 4
Pivot  : 5
Bigger : 8

Now sort the smaller part:

3 1 4

Pick pivot = 3

Smaller: 1
Pivot  : 3
Bigger : 4

Now combine:

1 3 4 5 8

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Choose a pivot
  2. Put smaller elements on one side
  3. Put bigger elements on the other side
  4. Recursively sort both sides

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
quickSort(arr):
    if array has 0 or 1 item:
        return

    choose pivot
    partition array into left and right
    quickSort(left)
    quickSort(right)

docs.title: Time Complexity
docs.text: Case Time Best O(n log n) Average O(n log n) Worst O(n^2)

docs.title: Best And Average Case: O(n log n)
docs.text:
If the pivot splits the array into two fairly equal parts:

  • there are about log n levels
  • each level processes about n elements

So:

n * log n

docs.title: Worst Case: O(n^2)
docs.text:
If the pivot is always terrible, like always the smallest or largest item, the split becomes very uneven.

Example:

1 2 3 4 5

If we always choose the first or last element as pivot, we get:

n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ...

That becomes O(n^2).

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text:
• Usually O(log n) extra stack space because of recursion
  • In the worst case, recursion stack can become O(n)

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Usually one of the fastest in real programs
  • Very popular
  • Can become slow with bad pivot choices

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text:
• Large arrays
  • General-purpose fast sorting
  • When average speed matters a lot

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
Imagine you want to line up kids from shortest to tallest.

You look at everyone, find the shortest kid, and place them first.

Then you look at the remaining kids, find the next shortest, and place them second.

That is Selection Sort.

docs.title: Small Example
docs.text:
Sort:

5 3 8 1

Step 1: find the smallest number, which is 1

5 3 8 1
1 3 8 5

Step 2: from the remaining part, find the smallest number, which is 3

1 3 8 5

Step 3: from the remaining part, find the smallest number, which is 5

1 3 5 8

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
Unsorted: [5] [3] [8] [1]
Smallest:              [1]
Swap with first:
[1] [3] [8] [5]

Now sort the rest:
[1] [3] [8] [5]
     sorted   unsorted

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Find the smallest element in the unsorted part
  2. Swap it with the first unsorted position
  3. Move the boundary of the sorted part one step right
  4. Repeat

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
selectionSort(arr):
    for i from 0 to n-2:
        minIndex = i
        for j from i+1 to n-1:
            if arr[j] < arr[minIndex]:
                minIndex = j
        swap arr[i], arr[minIndex]

docs.title: Time Complexity
docs.text: Case Time Best O(n^2) Average O(n^2) Worst O(n^2)

docs.title: Why This Time Complexity?
docs.text:
Selection Sort always searches the rest of the array to find the smallest item.

That means:

  • first round checks about n items
  • second round checks about n-1
  • then n-2
  • and so on

So total work is:

n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ...

That becomes about n^2, even if the list was already sorted.

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text: O(1) because it uses only a few extra variables.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Easy to understand
  • Usually slower than good O(n log n) sorts
  • Makes fewer swaps than Bubble Sort

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text:
• When swaps are expensive and you want to keep them low
  • When teaching the idea of choosing the smallest repeatedly

docs.title: Visual Comparison Of Sorting Algorithms
docs.text:
This file uses Mermaid diagrams to show how different sorting algorithms work.

If your Markdown viewer supports Mermaid, these diagrams will render as flowcharts.

docs.title: Bubble Sort
docs.text:
Idea: compare neighbors and swap if they are in the wrong order.

mermaid flowchart LR A[5 3 8 1] --> B[Compare 5 and 3] B --> C[Swap] C --> D[3 5 8 1] D --> E[Compare 5 and 8] E --> F[No Swap] F --> G[Compare 8 and 1] G --> H[Swap] H --> I[3 5 1 8]

docs.title: Insertion Sort
docs.text:
Idea: take one new item and insert it into the correct place in the sorted part.

mermaid flowchart LR A[Sorted: 5 | Unsorted: 3 8 1] --> B[Take 3] B --> C[Insert before 5] C --> D[Sorted: 3 5 | Unsorted: 8 1] D --> E[Take 8] E --> F[Insert after 5] F --> G[Sorted: 3 5 8 | Unsorted: 1]

docs.title: Selection Sort
docs.text:
Idea: find the smallest item and place it in front.

mermaid flowchart LR A[5 3 8 1] --> B[Find smallest] B --> C[Smallest is 1] C --> D[Swap with first element] D --> E[1 3 8 5] E --> F[Repeat on remaining part]

docs.title: Quick Sort
docs.text:
Idea: choose a pivot, split into smaller and bigger parts, then sort both sides.

mermaid flowchart TD A[5 3 8 1 4] --> B[Choose pivot 5] B --> C[Smaller: 3 1 4] B --> D[Pivot: 5] B --> E[Bigger: 8] C --> F[Choose pivot 3] F --> G[1] F --> H[3] F --> I[4] G --> J[1 3 4] H --> J I --> J J --> K[1 3 4 5 8] D --> K E --> K

docs.title: Merge Sort
docs.text:
Idea: split into halves, sort each half, then merge them back together.

mermaid flowchart TD A[5 3 8 1] --> B[5 3] A --> C[8 1] B --> D[5] B --> E[3] C --> F[8] C --> G[1] D --> H[3 5] E --> H F --> I[1 8] G --> I H --> J[1 3 5 8] I --> J

docs.title: Heap Sort
docs.text:
Idea: build a max-heap, remove the largest item, and repeat.

mermaid flowchart TD A[4 10 3 5 1] --> B[Build Max Heap] B --> C[Top is 10] C --> D[Swap 10 with last] D --> E[Fix heap] E --> F[Top is next largest] F --> G[Repeat]

docs.title: One-Page Comparison
docs.text:
```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sorting Algorithms] --> B[Bubble Sort] A --> C[Insertion Sort] A --> D[Selection Sort] A --> E[Quick Sort] A --> F[Merge Sort] A --> G[Heap Sort]

B --> B1[Swap neighbors]
B --> B2[Usually O(n^2)]

C --> C1[Insert into sorted part]
C --> C2[Good for small or nearly sorted data]

D --> D1[Pick smallest each round]
D --> D2[Always O(n^2)]

E --> E1[Split around pivot]
E --> E2[Average O(n log n)]
E --> E3[Worst O(n^2)]

F --> F1[Split and merge]
F --> F2[Always O(n log n)]
F --> F3[Needs extra memory]

G --> G1[Use max-heap]
G --> G2[Always O(n log n)]
G --> G3[In-place]

```

docs.text:
mermaid flowchart LR A[Usually Slower] --> B[Bubble] B --> C[Selection] C --> D[Insertion] D --> E[Heap] E --> F[Merge] F --> G[Quick] G --> H[Usually Faster]

docs.title: Stacks And Queues
docs.text:
Stacks and queues are linear data structures.

They both store items in order, but they remove items in different ways.

docs.title: Fast Idea
docs.text: Structure Rule Stack Last In, First Out (LIFO) Queue First In, First Out (FIFO) Priority Queue Higher-priority item gets served first

docs.title: Real-Life Picture
docs.text:
• Stack: pile of plates
  • Queue: line of people
  • Circular Queue: chairs around a round table
  • Priority Queue: emergency patients, where urgent cases go first

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
A queue is like a line of people waiting for tickets.

The person who comes first gets served first.

This rule is called:

FIFO = First In, First Out

docs.title: Queue ADT Operations
docs.text:
• enqueue(x) : insert at rear
  • dequeue() : remove from front
  • front() : see front item
  • rear() : see rear item
  • isEmpty() : check if queue has no items
  • isFull() : check if queue is full in array implementation
  • display() : show items

docs.title: Picture
docs.text:
Front                     Rear
 |                          |
 v                          v
[10] [20] [30] [40]

docs.title: Enqueue
docs.text:
enqueue(queue, x):
    if queue is full:
        overflow
    rear = rear + 1
    queue[rear] = x

docs.title: Dequeue
docs.text:
dequeue(queue):
    if queue is empty:
        underflow
    x = queue[front]
    front = front + 1
    return x

docs.title: Front
docs.text:
front(queue):
    if queue is empty:
        underflow
    return queue[front]

docs.title: Rear
docs.text:
rear(queue):
    if queue is empty:
        underflow
    return queue[rear]

docs.title: Complexity Analysis
docs.text: Operation Time Enqueue O(1) Dequeue O(1) Front O(1) Rear O(1) Is Empty O(1) Is Full O(1) Display O(n)

docs.title: Problem In Simple Array Queue
docs.text:
If front moves forward again and again, empty spaces at the beginning get wasted.

That is why circular queue is useful.

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
A stack is like a pile of books.

You can:

  • put a book on top
  • remove a book from the top

But you cannot remove the book from the middle first.

This rule is called:

LIFO = Last In, First Out

docs.title: Stack ADT Operations
docs.text:
• push(x) : add item on top
  • pop() : remove top item
  • peek() or top() : see top item
  • isEmpty() : check if no items exist
  • isFull() : check if stack is full in array implementation
  • display() : show all items

docs.title: Array Representation
docs.text:
Top
 |
 v
[10]
[20]
[30]

If top = -1, the stack is empty.

docs.title: Push
docs.text:
push(stack, x):
    if top == MAX - 1:
        overflow
    top = top + 1
    stack[top] = x

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Pop
docs.text:
pop(stack):
    if top == -1:
        underflow
    x = stack[top]
    top = top - 1
    return x

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Peek
docs.text:
peek(stack):
    if top == -1:
        underflow
    return stack[top]

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Is Empty
docs.text:
isEmpty():
    return top == -1

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Is Full
docs.text:
isFull():
    return top == MAX - 1

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Display
docs.text:
display(stack):
    for i from top down to 0:
        print stack[i]

Time: O(n)

docs.title: Complexity Analysis
docs.text: Operation Time Push O(1) Pop O(1) Peek O(1) Is Empty O(1) Is Full O(1) Display O(n)

docs.title: Why These Complexities?
docs.text:
• push, pop, and peek only touch the top
  • display must visit every element

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text:
• Array stack stores up to n items
  • total space is O(n)

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• very simple
  • fast insert and delete at one end
  • useful in many algorithms

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text:
• only one end is usable
  • array stack has fixed size unless resized

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
Imagine seats arranged in a circle.

After the last seat, you come back to the first seat.

That is how a circular queue works.

It reuses free spaces.

docs.title: Why It Is Better Than Simple Queue
docs.text:
In a simple queue, removed spaces in front may get wasted.

In a circular queue, rear can wrap around and use them again.

docs.title: Full And Empty Conditions
docs.text:
Queue is empty when:

front == -1

Queue is full when:

(rear + 1) % MAX == front

docs.title: Enqueue
docs.text:
enqueue(x):
    if (rear + 1) % MAX == front:
        overflow

    if front == -1:
        front = rear = 0
    else:
        rear = (rear + 1) % MAX

    queue[rear] = x

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Dequeue
docs.text:
dequeue():
    if front == -1:
        underflow

    x = queue[front]

    if front == rear:
        front = rear = -1
    else:
        front = (front + 1) % MAX

    return x

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Display
docs.text:
display():
    if front == -1:
        return

    i = front
    while true:
        print queue[i]
        if i == rear:
            break
        i = (i + 1) % MAX

Time: O(n)

docs.title: Complexity Summary
docs.text: Operation Time Enqueue O(1) Dequeue O(1) Front O(1) Rear O(1) Display O(n)

docs.title: Main Advantage
docs.text: Better memory usage than a simple array queue.

docs.title: Main Rule
docs.text: Structure Rule Stack Last In, First Out Queue First In, First Out Priority Queue Highest priority first

docs.title: Operation Comparison
docs.text: Structure Insert Delete Peek Stack push at top pop from top top element Simple Queue enqueue at rear dequeue from front front element Circular Queue enqueue at rear with wrap-around dequeue from front with wrap-around front element Priority Queue insert by priority rule remove highest-priority item highest-priority item

docs.title: Time Complexity Table
docs.text: Structure Insert Delete Peek Stack O(1) O(1) O(1) Simple Queue O(1) O(1) O(1) Circular Queue O(1) O(1) O(1) Priority Queue (unsorted array) O(1) O(n) O(n) Priority Queue (heap) O(log n) O(log n) O(1)

docs.title: When To Use What
docs.text:
Choose Stack if:

  • last inserted item should come out first
  • you are dealing with recursion, undo, brackets, or expressions

Choose Simple Queue if:

  • first entered item should leave first
  • array waste is not a problem

Choose Circular Queue if:

  • you want efficient reuse of array space

Choose Priority Queue if:

  • some items must be handled before others

docs.title: Stack Applications: Expression Conversion And Evaluation
docs.text:
Stacks are very useful in expressions like:

A + B * C

They help with:

  • expression conversion
  • expression evaluation
  • matching brackets
  • recursion and function calls

docs.title: Infix
docs.text:
Operator is between operands.

A + B

docs.title: Prefix
docs.text:
Operator comes before operands.

+ A B

docs.title: Postfix
docs.text:
Operator comes after operands.

A B +

docs.title: Why Prefix And Postfix Are Helpful
docs.text:
They avoid confusion about brackets and operator priority.

For example:

A + B * C

In postfix:

A B C * +

Now the order is clear.

docs.title: Main Idea
docs.text:
1. Read expression left to right
  2. Send operands directly to output
  3. Put operators on stack
  4. Pop higher-priority operators before pushing lower-priority ones
  5. Pop all remaining operators at the end

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
infixToPostfix(expr):
    create empty stack
    for each symbol in expr:
        if symbol is operand:
            add to output
        else if symbol is '(':
            push to stack
        else if symbol is ')':
            while top of stack is not '(':
                pop from stack to output
            pop '('
        else if symbol is operator:
            while stack not empty and precedence(top) >= precedence(symbol):
                pop from stack to output
            push symbol

    while stack not empty:
        pop from stack to output

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Time: O(n)

Why:

  • each symbol is pushed or popped at most once

Space: O(n)

docs.title: Infix To Prefix
docs.text:
One common method:

  1. reverse infix expression
  2. swap ( and )
  3. convert to postfix
  4. reverse result

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Time: O(n)

Space: O(n)

docs.title: Main Idea
docs.text:
1. Read from left to right
  2. If operand, push it
  3. If operator, pop two operands
  4. apply operation
  5. push result back

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
evaluatePostfix(expr):
    create empty stack
    for each symbol in expr:
        if symbol is operand:
            push symbol
        else:
            b = pop()
            a = pop()
            result = a operator b
            push result
    return pop()

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
2 3 4 * +

Process:

push 2
push 3
push 4
* => 3 * 4 = 12
push 12
+ => 2 + 12 = 14

Answer: 14

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Time: O(n)

Space: O(n)

docs.title: Main Idea
docs.text:
Read from right to left.

If operand, push it.

If operator, pop two values, apply operator, push result.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
evaluatePrefix(expr):
    create empty stack
    for each symbol from right to left:
        if symbol is operand:
            push symbol
        else:
            a = pop()
            b = pop()
            result = symbol applied to a and b
            push result
    return pop()

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Time: O(n)

Space: O(n)

docs.title: Other Applications Of Stacks
docs.text:
• function call management
  • undo and redo
  • browser backtracking
  • depth-first search
  • balancing parentheses

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text: Task Time Space Infix to Postfix O(n) O(n) Infix to Prefix O(n) O(n) Postfix Evaluation O(n) O(n) Prefix Evaluation O(n) O(n)

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
In a normal queue, the first person in line goes first.

In a priority queue, a more important person may go first.

Example:

  • emergency patient first
  • regular patient later

So removal depends on priority, not just arrival time.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Each item has:

  • data
  • priority

Example:

(A, 3), (B, 1), (C, 2)

If smaller number means higher priority, then B leaves first.

docs.title: Common Operations
docs.text:
• insert item with priority
  • delete highest-priority item
  • peek highest-priority item

docs.title: Insert
docs.text:
If unsorted array is used:

insert(x, p):
    add (x, p) at the end

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Delete Highest Priority
docs.text:
deletePriority():
    find highest-priority item
    remove it
    shift remaining items if needed

Time: O(n)

docs.title: Peek Highest Priority
docs.text:
peekPriority():
    scan all items and return highest-priority one

Time: O(n)

docs.title: Sorted Array Method
docs.text:
If the array is kept sorted by priority:

  • insertion becomes O(n)
  • deletion from front may become O(1)

docs.title: Heap-Based Priority Queue
docs.text:
Using a heap:

  • insertion: O(log n)
  • deletion of highest priority: O(log n)
  • peek: O(1)

This is the most common efficient implementation.

docs.title: Complexity Summary
docs.text: Implementation Insert Delete Highest Priority Peek Unsorted Array O(1) O(n) O(n) Sorted Array O(n) O(1) O(1) Heap O(log n) O(log n) O(1)

docs.title: Applications
docs.text:
• CPU scheduling
  • printer task handling
  • network packet handling
  • graph algorithms like Dijkstra and Prim

docs.title: Simple Queue
docs.text: A simple queue is the normal queue implemented using an array or linked list.

docs.title: Array Representation
docs.text:
Use two variables:

  • front
  • rear

Example:

Index: 0   1   2   3   4
Data : 10  20  30  _   _
        ^       ^
      front    rear

docs.title: Enqueue
docs.text:
enqueue(x):
    if rear == MAX - 1:
        overflow
    if front == -1:
        front = 0
    rear = rear + 1
    queue[rear] = x

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Dequeue
docs.text:
dequeue():
    if front == -1 or front > rear:
        underflow
    x = queue[front]
    front = front + 1
    return x

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Peek Front
docs.text:
peekFront():
    return queue[front]

Time: O(1)

docs.title: Display
docs.text:
display():
    for i from front to rear:
        print queue[i]

Time: O(n)

docs.title: Complexity Summary
docs.text: Operation Time Enqueue O(1) Dequeue O(1) Peek Front O(1) Display O(n)

docs.title: Weakness
docs.text:
After several dequeues, the front moves right and free spots on the left cannot be reused easily in the simple array version.

This waste is called false overflow.

docs.title: Trees
docs.text:
A tree is a structure made of nodes connected like a family tree.

It starts from a top node called the root.

docs.title: Fast Idea
docs.text: Tree Main Idea Binary Tree Each node has at most 2 children Threaded Binary Tree Empty child links are used to help traversal BST Left smaller, right larger AVL Tree BST that keeps itself balanced B-Tree Multi-child balanced tree for disks/databases B+ Tree Like B-Tree, but data search is optimized in leaves

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
For side-by-side notes, read Comparison.

For diagram-heavy notes, read Visual Comparison.

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
An AVL tree is a BST that keeps itself balanced.

It does not allow one side to become much taller than the other.

docs.title: Balance Rule
docs.text:
For every node:

balance factor = height(left) - height(right)

Allowed values:

  • -1
  • 0
  • 1

If the balance factor goes outside that range, the tree rotates to fix itself.

docs.title: LL Rotation
docs.text: Right rotation

docs.title: RR Rotation
docs.text: Left rotation

docs.title: LR Rotation
docs.text: Left then right rotation

docs.title: RL Rotation
docs.text: Right then left rotation

docs.title: Insert
docs.text:
1. Insert like BST
  2. Update heights
  3. Check balance factor
  4. Rotate if needed

docs.title: Delete
docs.text:
1. Delete like BST
  2. Update heights
  3. Rebalance using rotations

docs.title: Complexity Analysis
docs.text: Operation Time Search O(log n) Insert O(log n) Delete O(log n)

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: AVL keeps height near log n, so operations travel only along a short root-to-leaf path.

docs.title: Main Advantage
docs.text: Faster worst-case searching than a plain BST.

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text: Insert and delete are more complex because rotations may be needed.

docs.title: Applications Of Binary Trees
docs.text: Binary trees are not just classroom ideas. They are used in many places.

docs.title: Expression Trees
docs.text:
Used to represent expressions like:

(A + B) * C

Operators become internal nodes and operands become leaves.

docs.title: Binary Search Trees
docs.text: Used for searching, inserting, and deleting data efficiently.

docs.title: Heaps
docs.text: Used in priority queues and heap sort.

docs.title: Huffman Coding Trees
docs.text: Used in data compression.

docs.title: Decision Trees
docs.text: Used in AI and machine learning.

docs.title: Syntax Trees
docs.text: Used by compilers to understand code.

docs.title: File And Folder Hierarchy
docs.text: Tree idea helps represent hierarchical systems.

docs.title: Why Binary Trees Are Popular
docs.text:
• natural left/right structure
  • easy recursive processing
  • useful for searching and hierarchy

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
A B+ Tree is like a B-Tree, but it stores actual record access mainly in the leaf nodes.

Internal nodes mostly guide the search.

Leaves are often linked together, which makes range searching very fast.

docs.title: Main Properties
docs.text:
• balanced multiway tree
  • internal nodes store guide keys
  • leaf nodes store data entries
  • leaves are linked in order

docs.title: Search
docs.text:
1. start at root
  2. move down using guide keys
  3. reach a leaf
  4. find the key there

docs.title: Insertion
docs.text:
1. insert key into correct leaf
  2. if leaf overflows, split it
  3. copy separator upward
  4. continue if parent overflows

docs.title: Deletion
docs.text:
1. remove key from leaf
  2. if underflow happens, borrow or merge
  3. update parent separators if needed

docs.title: Complexity Analysis
docs.text: Operation Time Search O(log n) Insert O(log n) Delete O(log n) Range Search very efficient

docs.title: Why B+ Tree Is So Popular
docs.text:
• all data appears in leaves
  • leaf links make ordered scanning easy
  • great for database indexes

docs.title: B-Tree Vs B+ Tree Quick Idea
docs.text:
• B-Tree: data may appear in internal and leaf nodes
  • B+ Tree: search path goes to leaves, and leaves are linked

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
A B-Tree is a balanced search tree where one node can hold many keys, not just one.

This makes it very useful for disks and databases because it reduces how often we must go to storage.

docs.title: Main Properties
docs.text:
• a node can have many children
  • keys inside a node stay sorted
  • all leaves are at the same level
  • tree stays balanced

docs.title: Why Not Just Use BST?
docs.text:
Disk access is slow.

B-Tree keeps the tree short, so fewer disk reads are needed.

docs.title: Search
docs.text:
1. look inside current node
  2. if key is found, stop
  3. otherwise move to correct child
  4. repeat

docs.title: Insertion
docs.text:
1. go to correct leaf
  2. insert key in sorted order
  3. if node overflows, split it
  4. move middle key up
  5. repeat upward if needed

docs.title: Deletion
docs.text:
Deletion is more involved.

Common ideas:

  • remove from leaf if possible
  • if a node gets too small, borrow from sibling
  • or merge with sibling

docs.title: Complexity Analysis
docs.text: Operation Time Search O(log n) Insert O(log n) Delete O(log n)

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
The tree remains balanced and very short.

Height is logarithmic in the number of keys.

docs.title: Applications
docs.text:
• databases
  • file systems
  • indexing large records

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
A BST is a binary tree with a special rule:

  • left side has smaller values
  • right side has larger values

That rule makes searching faster.

docs.title: Search
docs.text:
search(root, key):
    if root == NULL or root.data == key:
        return root
    if key < root.data:
        return search(root.left, key)
    else:
        return search(root.right, key)

docs.title: Insert
docs.text:
insert(root, key):
    if root == NULL:
        return new node(key)
    if key < root.data:
        root.left = insert(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.data:
        root.right = insert(root.right, key)
    return root

docs.title: Delete
docs.text:
Three cases:

  1. node is a leaf
  2. node has one child
  3. node has two children

For case 3, replace with inorder successor or predecessor.

delete(root, key):
    if root == NULL:
        return NULL

    if key < root.data:
        root.left = delete(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.data:
        root.right = delete(root.right, key)
    else:
        if root.left == NULL:
            return root.right
        if root.right == NULL:
            return root.left

        temp = minimum(root.right)
        root.data = temp.data
        root.right = delete(root.right, temp.data)
    return root

docs.title: Traversal
docs.text: Inorder traversal of BST gives sorted order.

docs.title: Complexity Analysis
docs.text: Operation Average Worst Search O(log n) O(n) Insert O(log n) O(n) Delete O(log n) O(n)

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
• if the tree stays balanced, height is about log n
  • if it becomes skewed like a chain, height becomes n

Example of bad case:

10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40

That is why self-balancing trees are useful.

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
A binary tree is a tree where each node can have:

  • no child
  • one child
  • or two children

The two sides are called:

  • left child
  • right child

docs.title: Picture

docs.title: Full Binary Tree
docs.text: Every node has either 0 or 2 children.

docs.title: Complete Binary Tree
docs.text: All levels are full except maybe the last, and the last fills from left to right.

docs.title: Preorder
docs.text:
Root, Left, Right

preorder(node):
    if node == NULL:
        return
    visit node
    preorder(node.left)
    preorder(node.right)

docs.title: Inorder
docs.text:
Left, Root, Right

inorder(node):
    if node == NULL:
        return
    inorder(node.left)
    visit node
    inorder(node.right)

docs.title: Postorder
docs.text:
Left, Right, Root

postorder(node):
    if node == NULL:
        return
    postorder(node.left)
    postorder(node.right)
    visit node

docs.title: Level Order
docs.text:
Uses queue.

levelOrder(root):
    if root == NULL:
        return
    enqueue(root)
    while queue not empty:
        node = dequeue()
        visit node
        if node.left != NULL:
            enqueue(node.left)
        if node.right != NULL:
            enqueue(node.right)

docs.title: Insertion In General Binary Tree
docs.text:
One common method inserts at the first empty place in level order.

insert(root, x):
    newNode = create node
    if root == NULL:
        root = newNode
        return

    enqueue(root)
    while queue not empty:
        temp = dequeue()
        if temp.left == NULL:
            temp.left = newNode
            return
        else:
            enqueue(temp.left)

        if temp.right == NULL:
            temp.right = newNode
            return
        else:
            enqueue(temp.right)

docs.title: Deletion In General Binary Tree
docs.text:
One common method:

  1. find node to delete
  2. find deepest rightmost node
  3. copy deepest node's data
  4. delete deepest node

In a normal binary tree there is no ordering rule like BST.

So you may need to inspect many nodes.

docs.title: Why Trees Are Useful
docs.text:
• organize hierarchical data
  • fast searching in good tree types
  • used in file systems, databases, compilers, and AI

docs.title: Idea Like You Are 10
docs.text:
In a normal binary tree, many child pointers are empty.

A threaded binary tree uses some of those empty pointers to point to the next node in traversal order.

This helps traversal happen without stack or recursion in some cases.

docs.title: Simple Idea
docs.text:
In an inorder threaded tree:

  • if a node has no right child
  • its right pointer may point to its inorder successor

That special pointer is called a thread.

docs.title: Why Use It?
docs.text:
• easier traversal
  • less need for recursion stack

docs.title: Inorder Traversal Idea
docs.text:
inorderThreaded(root):
    go to leftmost node
    while current != NULL:
        visit current
        if current has thread:
            current = current.right
        else:
            current = leftmost node of current.right

docs.title: Traversal
docs.text: Time: O(n)

docs.title: Search
docs.text: Time: O(n)

docs.title: Insertion
docs.text:
Insertion must carefully update:

  • normal child links
  • thread links

Time: usually O(h) after reaching the place, where h is tree height.

docs.title: Deletion
docs.text:
Deletion is more complex because threads must be fixed.

Time: usually O(h) plus thread adjustment work.

docs.title: Complexity Summary
docs.text: Operation Time Traversal O(n) Search O(n) Insert O(h) Delete O(h)

docs.title: Main Advantage
docs.text: Traversal becomes more efficient in terms of auxiliary space.

docs.title: Space Note
docs.text: Traversal can be done with O(1) extra space in the threaded approach.

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#define SIZE 5

int iterativeBinarySearch(int arr[], int size, int key)
{
    int start = 0;
    int end = size - 1;

    while (start < end)
    {
        int mid = (start + end) / 2;

        if (arr[mid] == key)
        {
            return mid;
        }
        else if (arr[mid] < key)
        {
            start = mid + 1;
        }
        else
        {
            end = mid - 1;
        }
    }
    return -1;
}

int recursiveBinarySearch(int arr[], int size, int start, int end, int key)
{
    int mid = (start + end) / 2;

    if (arr[mid] == key)
    {
        return mid;
    }
    else if (arr[mid] < key)
    {
        start = mid + 1;
        return recursiveBinarySearch(arr, size, start, end, key);
    }
    else
    {
        end = mid - 1;
        return recursiveBinarySearch(arr, size, start, end, key);
    }
    return -1;
}

int main()
{
    int key, size, choice;
    int arr[SIZE] = {1, 4, 7, 8, 9};

    printf("Binary Search ~\n");
    printf("Enter Type Of Search ~\n1. Iterative\n2. Recursive\n");

    printf("Enter The Type Of Array ( 1 | 2 ) : ");
    scanf("%d", &choice);

    if (choice == 1)
    {
        printf("Enter The Element To Search : ");
        scanf("%d", &key);

        int elementIndex = iterativeBinarySearch(arr, SIZE, key);

        if (elementIndex != -1)
        {
            printf("Element Found At %d", elementIndex);
        }
        else
        {
            printf("Element Not Found!");
        }
    }
    else if (choice == 2)
    {

        printf("Enter The Element To Search : ");
        scanf("%d", &key);

        int start = 0;
        int end = SIZE - 1;

        int elementIndex = recursiveBinarySearch(arr, SIZE, start, end, key);

        if (elementIndex != -1)
        {
            printf("Element Found At %d", elementIndex);
        }
        else
        {
            printf("Element Not Found!");
        }
    }
    else
    {
        printf("Invalid Choice!");
    }

    return 0;
}

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

// Node Structure Definition
struct Node
{
    int Data;          // Data Field Of Node
    struct Node *Next; // Pointer To Next Node
};

// Global Head Pointer
struct Node *Head = NULL;

// Function To Create New Node
struct Node *CreateNode(int Value)
{
    struct Node *NewNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); // Allocate Memory
    NewNode->Data = Value;                                             // Assign Data
    NewNode->Next = NULL;                                              // Initialize Next Pointer
    return NewNode;
}

// Insertion At Beginning
void InsertAtFirst(int Value)
{
    struct Node *NewNode = CreateNode(Value); // Create New Node

    if (Head == NULL) // If List Is Empty
    {
        Head = NewNode;
        NewNode->Next = Head; // Point To Itself
        return;
    }

    struct Node *Temp = Head; // Temporary Pointer

    while (Temp->Next != Head) // Traverse Till Last Node
    {
        Temp = Temp->Next;
    }

    Temp->Next = NewNode; // Last Node Points To New Node
    NewNode->Next = Head; // New Node Points To Old Head
    Head = NewNode;       // Update Head
}

// Insertion At End
void InsertAtEnd(int Value)
{
    struct Node *NewNode = CreateNode(Value); // Create New Node

    if (Head == NULL) // If List Is Empty
    {
        Head = NewNode;
        NewNode->Next = Head;
        return;
    }

    struct Node *Temp = Head;

    while (Temp->Next != Head) // Traverse Till Last Node
    {
        Temp = Temp->Next;
    }

    Temp->Next = NewNode; // Last Node Points To New Node
    NewNode->Next = Head; // Maintain Circular Property
}

// Insertion At Nth Position
void InsertAtPosition(int Value, int Position)
{
    if (Position == 1)
    {
        InsertAtFirst(Value);
        return;
    }

    struct Node *NewNode = CreateNode(Value);
    struct Node *Temp = Head;

    for (int i = 1; i < Position - 1 && Temp->Next != Head; i++)
    {
        Temp = Temp->Next;
    }

    NewNode->Next = Temp->Next;
    Temp->Next = NewNode;
}

// Deletion At Beginning
void DeleteAtFirst()
{
    if (Head == NULL) // If List Is Empty
    {
        printf("List Is Empty\n");
        return;
    }

    if (Head->Next == Head) // Only One Node
    {
        free(Head);
        Head = NULL;
        return;
    }

    struct Node *Temp = Head;

    while (Temp->Next != Head) // Find Last Node
    {
        Temp = Temp->Next;
    }

    struct Node *DeleteNode = Head;
    Temp->Next = Head->Next; // Last Node Points To Second Node
    Head = Head->Next;       // Update Head
    free(DeleteNode);        // Free Memory
}

// Deletion At End
void DeleteAtEnd()
{
    if (Head == NULL)
    {
        printf("List Is Empty\n");
        return;
    }

    if (Head->Next == Head) // Only One Node
    {
        free(Head);
        Head = NULL;
        return;
    }

    struct Node *Temp = Head;
    struct Node *Prev = NULL;

    while (Temp->Next != Head)
    {
        Prev = Temp;
        Temp = Temp->Next;
    }

    Prev->Next = Head; // Second Last Points To Head
    free(Temp);        // Delete Last Node
}

// Deletion At Nth Position
void DeleteAtPosition(int Position)
{
    if (Head == NULL)
    {
        printf("List Is Empty\n");
        return;
    }

    if (Position == 1)
    {
        DeleteAtFirst();
        return;
    }

    struct Node *Temp = Head;
    struct Node *Prev = NULL;

    for (int i = 1; i < Position && Temp->Next != Head; i++)
    {
        Prev = Temp;
        Temp = Temp->Next;
    }

    Prev->Next = Temp->Next;
    free(Temp);
}

// Display Circular Linked List
void Display()
{
    if (Head == NULL)
    {
        printf("List Is Empty\n");
        return;
    }

    struct Node *Temp = Head;

    do
    {
        printf("%d -> ", Temp->Data);
        Temp = Temp->Next;
    } while (Temp != Head);

    printf("(Back To Head)\n");
}

// Main Function For Testing
int main()
{
    InsertAtFirst(10);
    InsertAtFirst(5);
    InsertAtEnd(20);
    InsertAtEnd(30);

    Display();

    InsertAtPosition(15, 3);
    Display();

    DeleteAtFirst();
    Display();

    DeleteAtEnd();
    Display();

    DeleteAtPosition(2);
    Display();

    return 0;
}

#include <stdio.h>
#define SIZE 5

// Global Queue Array And Pointers
int Queue[SIZE]; // Array To Store Elements
int Front = -1;  // Points To First Element
int Rear = -1;   // Points To Last Element

// Function To Check If Queue Is Full
int IsFull()
{
    if ((Front == 0 && Rear == SIZE - 1) || (Front == Rear + 1))
    {
        return 1; // Queue Is Full
    }
    return 0; // Queue Is Not Full
}

// Function To Check If Queue Is Empty
int IsEmpty()
{
    if (Front == -1)
    {
        return 1; // Queue Is Empty
    }
    return 0; // Queue Is Not Empty
}

// Function To Insert Element
void Push(int Value)
{
    if (IsFull())
    {
        printf("Queue Is Full\n");
        return;
    }

    if (Front == -1) // First Element In Queue
    {
        Front = 0;
        Rear = 0;
    }
    else if (Rear == SIZE - 1) // Circular Condition
    {
        Rear = 0;
    }
    else
    {
        Rear++;
    }

    Queue[Rear] = Value; // Insert Value
}

// Function To Delete Element
void Pop()
{
    if (IsEmpty())
    {
        printf("Queue Is Empty\n");
        return;
    }

    printf("Deleted Element Is %d\n", Queue[Front]);

    if (Front == Rear) // Only One Element
    {
        Front = -1;
        Rear = -1;
    }
    else if (Front == SIZE - 1) // Circular Condition
    {
        Front = 0;
    }
    else
    {
        Front++;
    }
}

// Function To Display Queue
void Display()
{
    if (IsEmpty())
    {
        printf("Queue Is Empty\n");
        return;
    }

    int i = Front;

    printf("Queue Elements Are : ");

    while (1)
    {
        printf("%d ", Queue[i]);

        if (i == Rear)
        {
            break;
        }

        if (i == SIZE - 1)
        {
            i = 0; // Circular Move
        }
        else
        {
            i++;
        }
    }

    printf("\n");
}

// Main Function For Testing
int main()
{
    Push(10);
    Push(20);
    Push(30);
    Push(40);
    Push(50);

    Display();

    Pop();
    Pop();

    Display();

    Push(60);
    Push(70);

    Display();

    return 0;
}

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node
{
    int data;
    struct Node *next; // Basically Name Is next
};

struct Node *head = NULL, *last = NULL;

void insertNodeLast() // Also Known As Append Node
{
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));

    printf("\nEnter Data : ");
    scanf("%d", newNode->data);

    newNode->next = NULL;

    if (head == NULL)
    {
        head = newNode;
        last = newNode;
    }
    else
    {
        last->next = newNode;
        last = newNode;
    }
}

void insertNode()
{
    int pos, counter = 1;

    printf("\nEnter The Position To Insert Node : ");
    scanf("%d", &pos);

    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));

    printf("\nEnter Data : ");
    scanf("%d", newNode->data);

    if (pos == 1)
    {
        newNode->next = head;
        head = newNode;

        if (last == NULL)
        {
            last = newNode;
        }
        return;
    }

    struct Node *tempNode = head;

    while (tempNode != NULL && counter < pos - 1)
    {
        tempNode = tempNode->next;
        counter++;
    }

    if (newNode->next == NULL)
    {
        last = newNode;
    }

    newNode->next = tempNode->next;
    tempNode->next = newNode;
}

void deleteNode()
{
    int pos, counter = 1;

    printf("\nEnter The Position To Delete Node : ");
    scanf("%d", &pos);

    struct Node *temp = head;
    struct Node *prev = NULL;

    if (pos == 1)
    {
        head = head->next;
        free(temp);

        if (head == NULL)
        {
            last == NULL;
        }

        return;
    }

    while (temp != NULL && counter < pos)
    {
        prev = temp;
        temp = temp->next;
        counter++;
    }

    prev->next = temp->next;

    if (temp->next == NULL)
        last = prev;

    free(temp);
}

void reverseNodes()
{

    struct Node *prev = NULL;
    struct Node *current = head;
    struct Node *next = NULL;

    while (current != NULL)
    {
        next = current->next; // Current Order
        current->next = prev; // Setting Current's Next To Be Previous Node
        prev = current;       // Now Previous Node Becomes Current
        current = next;       // Current Node Becomes Next
    }
}

#include <stdio.h>

// Function To Print Array
void PrintArray(int Arr[], int N)
{
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++)
    {
        printf("%d ", Arr[i]);
    }
    printf("\n");
}

// Bubble Sort
void BubbleSort(int Arr[], int N)
{
    // Outer Loop For Passes
    for (int i = 0; i < N - 1; i++)
    {
        // Inner Loop For Comparison
        for (int j = 0; j < N - i - 1; j++)
        {
            if (Arr[j] > Arr[j + 1]) // Swap If Greater
            {
                int Temp = Arr[j];
                Arr[j] = Arr[j + 1];
                Arr[j + 1] = Temp;
            }
        }
    }
}

// Selection Sort
void SelectionSort(int Arr[], int N)
{
    // Loop For Selecting Minimum Element
    for (int i = 0; i < N - 1; i++)
    {
        int MinIndex = i; // Assume Current Index Is Minimum

        for (int j = i + 1; j < N; j++)
        {
            if (Arr[j] < Arr[MinIndex])
            {
                MinIndex = j; // Update Minimum Index
            }
        }

        // Swap Minimum With Current Position
        int Temp = Arr[i];
        Arr[i] = Arr[MinIndex];
        Arr[MinIndex] = Temp;
    }
}

// Insertion Sort
void InsertionSort(int Arr[], int N)
{
    for (int i = 1; i < N; i++)
    {
        int Key = Arr[i]; // Current Element
        int j = i - 1;

        // Shift Elements Greater Than Key
        while (j >= 0 && Arr[j] > Key)
        {
            Arr[j + 1] = Arr[j];
            j--;
        }

        Arr[j + 1] = Key; // Insert Key At Correct Position
    }
}

// Partition Function For Quick Sort
int Partition(int Arr[], int Low, int High)
{
    int Pivot = Arr[High]; // Choose Last Element As Pivot
    int i = Low - 1;       // Index Of Smaller Element

    for (int j = Low; j < High; j++)
    {
        if (Arr[j] < Pivot)
        {
            i++;

            // Swap Elements
            int Temp = Arr[i];
            Arr[i] = Arr[j];
            Arr[j] = Temp;
        }
    }

    // Place Pivot At Correct Position
    int Temp = Arr[i + 1];
    Arr[i + 1] = Arr[High];
    Arr[High] = Temp;

    return i + 1;
}

// Quick Sort
void QuickSort(int Arr[], int Low, int High)
{
    if (Low < High)
    {
        int PivotIndex = Partition(Arr, Low, High);

        // Recursive Calls For Subarrays
        QuickSort(Arr, Low, PivotIndex - 1);
        QuickSort(Arr, PivotIndex + 1, High);
    }
}

// Main Function For Testing
int main()
{
    int Arr1[] = {5, 2, 9, 1, 6};
    int Arr2[] = {5, 2, 9, 1, 6};
    int Arr3[] = {5, 2, 9, 1, 6};
    int Arr4[] = {5, 2, 9, 1, 6};
    int N = 5;

    printf("Bubble Sort:\n");
    BubbleSort(Arr1, N);
    PrintArray(Arr1, N);

    printf("Selection Sort:\n");
    SelectionSort(Arr2, N);
    PrintArray(Arr2, N);

    printf("Insertion Sort:\n");
    InsertionSort(Arr3, N);
    PrintArray(Arr3, N);

    printf("Quick Sort:\n");
    QuickSort(Arr4, 0, N - 1);
    PrintArray(Arr4, N);

    return 0;
}
